moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Zukertort Opening, named after the 19th-century grandmaster Wilhelm Zukertort—a flexible, hypermodern approach that avoids committing the center immediately. By developing our knight to f3, we control the key central squares e5 and d4 without placing a pawn there yet, keeping Black guessing about our true plan. This move also prepares to support a future pawn advance (like e4 or d4) and gets our knight to an active square in one move. The beauty of 1. Nf3 is its flexibility: we can transition into many different setups depending on Black's response.

Black mirrors our development with the natural 1...Nc6, developing a piece while mirroring our knight setup and controlling the important d4 and e5 squares from Black's perspective. This is the Black Mustang Defense—an assertive, flexible response that doesn't commit to any particular pawn structure yet and keeps multiple options open. By playing symmetrically, Black avoids falling into prepared lines while maintaining flexibility to respond to our next move. The name "Mustang" suggests the wild, free-spirited nature of this symmetrical, non-committal approach.
